task: I'm preparing a literature review on 'transformer attention mechanisms' for a grant proposal. Please search arXiv for the 50 most recent papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' in the cs.LG category, then identify the paper with the highest number of authors listed (if tied, pick the one appearing first in the results). Download that paper, read its full content, and extract the exact title of the first cited reference (i.e., reference [1]) from its reference list. Then search PubMed for papers matching that exact reference title as keywords (use up to 10 results), and report: (1) the arXiv paper ID you downloaded, (2) the exact title of reference [1] you found, and (3) the PMID of the first PubMed result returned for that search (or 'No results' if none found). Save these three pieces of information as a JSON file at /data/literature_review_signals.json with keys 'arxiv_paper_id', 'reference_1_title', and 'pubmed_first_pmid'.

state_assertions.description: Output file /data/literature_review_signals.json was created
Output file is non-empty (greater than 10 bytes)
JSON file contains all three required keys: arxiv_paper_id, reference_1_title, pubmed_first_pmid
arxiv_paper_id field is a non-empty string
reference_1_title field is a non-empty string
pubmed_first_pmid field is present and is either a numeric string or 'No results'

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: arXiv search returns up to 50 papers each with multiple metadata fields (title, authors, abstract, dates, categories, IDs); the full paper text is very long with many sections, figures, tables, and references; PubMed results include multiple articles with full metadata. The agent must identify the paper with the most authors from a large list, then locate reference [1] buried deep in a long paper, then correctly pass that title to PubMed search.

claims.description: Search arXiv for up to 50 papers on 'transformer attention mechanisms' in cs.LG category — returns large noisy list of papers each with many metadata fields
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper objects each containing: paper_id, title, authors (list), abstract, categories, published date, updated date, links — several hundred fields total across all results

claims.description: Identify which paper has the highest number of authors from the 50 results — must count authors for each paper and track the paper ID of the one with the most authors
claims.extraction_criteria: For each paper in results, count len(authors). Pick the paper_id where author count is maximum. If tied, take first occurrence.
claims.signal_to_remember: paper_id of the paper with the most authors

claims.description: Download the identified paper using the remembered paper_id
Read the full content of the downloaded paper — produces a very long noisy markdown document with abstract, introduction, methods, results, and references section
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: potentially thousands of lines including sections, equations, tables, figure captions, and a reference list. Agent must locate [1] in the references section specifically.

claims.description: Extract the title of reference [1] from the paper's reference list — the signal is buried at the end of a long document after many noisy sections
claims.extraction_criteria: Locate the References section of the paper, find the entry labeled [1], extract its title

claims.description: Search PubMed using the extracted reference [1] title as keywords — returns up to 10 PubMed results each with PMID, title, authors, journal, abstract
claims.output_characteristics: Up to 10 PubMed article metadata objects, each with PMID, title, authors, journal info

claims.description: Write the three collected signals to /data/literature_review_signals.json as a JSON object
claims.uses_signal: arxiv_paper_id (from step 2), reference_1_title (from step 5), pubmed_first_pmid (from step 6)

_design_rationale: (1) Chain length: 7 steps minimum — arXiv search, author-count analysis, download, read, reference extraction, PubMed search, file write — ensuring Medium difficulty (6-8 calls). (2) Noise sources: Step 1 returns 50 papers each with many metadata fields; Step 4 returns a full academic paper in markdown (thousands of lines with equations, tables, citations); Step 6 returns 10 PubMed records with full metadata. (3) Signal extraction: From step 1, the agent must count author lists across 50 papers and remember the paper_id with the highest count — this is non-obvious and buried in a large list. From step 4, the agent must parse to the references section at the end of a long paper and extract exactly the first reference title. (4) Signal usage: The paper_id from step 2 is used in step 3. The reference title from step 5 is used as the PubMed query in step 6. All three signals are used in the final write step 7. (5) Failure modes: If the agent picks the wrong paper (not max authors), downloads a different paper, and gets a different reference [1] title, the entire chain produces wrong results. If the agent confuses reference [2] or [3] for [1], the PubMed search fails or returns wrong results. (6) Difficulty match: 7+ tool calls, 3 distinct signals to track, large noisy intermediate outputs requiring careful parsing — fits Medium difficulty.

task: I'm researching NVIDIA's stock performance. First, pull the last 90 daily candles for NVDA from 2024-01-01 to 2024-04-30 using the time series endpoint. From that price history, identify the single trading day with the highest closing price — note that date and its closing price. Then fetch the current real-time quote for NVDA and report: (1) the date and closing price of that highest-close day found in the historical data, (2) the current real-time price from the quote, and (3) the percentage difference between the historical high-close price and today's current price (computed as ((current_price - historical_high_close) / historical_high_close) * 100).

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: GetTimeSeries returns up to 90 OHLCV candles, each with datetime/open/high/low/close/volume fields; agent must scan all rows to find the single maximum close and remember both its date and value across subsequent noisy steps before using them in the final percentage calculation.

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeries for NVDA with interval=1day, start_date=2024-01-01, end_date=2024-04-30, outputsize=90. Returns ~82 trading-day candles each with datetime, open, high, low, close, volume fields — a large noisy array.
claims.output_characteristics: ~82 OHLCV objects; only the 'close' and 'datetime' fields of the single maximum-close row are relevant; all others are noise.

claims.description: Scan all returned candles to find the one with the highest closing price. Extract and remember: (a) the date string of that candle, (b) the closing price as a float. This is the critical signal buried in ~82 rows of noise.
claims.extraction_criteria: Iterate values array, compare float(candle['close']) across all rows, keep the row where close is maximum.

claims.description: Call twelvedata_GetTimeSeriesRsi for NVDA to retrieve RSI data — this is a noise step that produces 30 RSI data points irrelevant to the final answer. The agent must not confuse RSI values with close prices.

Call twelvedata_GetQuote for NVDA to get the current full quote object. Returns many fields (open, high, low, close, volume, percent_change, 52-week range, etc.) — mostly noise. Extract the current price.
Call twelvedata_GetPrice for NVDA to get lightweight real-time price. Use this as the definitive current price.

Using the remembered signal from step 2 (highest_close_date and highest_close_price) plus the current price from step 5, compute the final percentage difference: ((current - highest_close) / highest_close) * 100. Report all three required values.

_design_rationale: 1) Chain length: 4+ tool calls (GetTimeSeries, GetTimeSeriesRsi as noise, GetQuote, GetPrice) naturally arise from a real analyst workflow: get historical data, optionally inspect technical indicators, then compare to today's price. 2) Noise sources: GetTimeSeries returns ~82 candles each with 6 fields (open/high/low/close/volume/datetime); the agent must scan all rows rather than picking an arbitrary one. GetQuote returns ~20+ fields per instrument, almost all irrelevant. GetTimeSeriesRsi adds 30 RSI objects that share the same 'datetime' key as the candles, creating confusion. 3) Signal extraction: The agent must correctly identify the single candle with the maximum close value from ~82 rows, remembering both its exact date string and float price. 4) Signal used later: This date and price are only used in the final percentage calculation after two more noisy tool calls (RSI + quote). 5) Failure mode: If agent forgets the extracted highest-close value or uses a wrong candle (e.g., the most recent one or the first one), the percentage calculation will be wrong. 6) Difficulty match: Easy (4-5 tool calls, 1 signal to extract and carry, ~80 items of noise) — straightforward linear chain with one filtering step.

task: I'm auditing our supplier data in the 'Supply Chain Analytics' base. Please look up all suppliers in the 'Supplier Registry' table, find the single supplier with the lowest QualityScore among those whose RiskLevel is 'High', note their SupplierID, then fetch that specific record's full details and report the supplier's SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, and DeliveryOnTime_Pct.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to find the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'
    execution_log.append("Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to discover base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'")
    raw1 = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}")
    bases_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f"Bases result sample: {str(bases_data)[:400]}... [truncated]")

    # Extract base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'
    bases_list = bases_data.get('bases', bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else [])
    supply_chain_base_id = None
    for base in bases_list:
        name = base.get('name', '')
        if 'Supply Chain Analytics' in name:
            supply_chain_base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', None))
            break
    execution_log.append(f"Extracted base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics': {supply_chain_base_id}")

    if not supply_chain_base_id:
        execution_log.append("ERROR: Could not find 'Supply Chain Analytics' base.")
        return "\n".join(execution_log)

    # Step 2: List tables in the base to confirm 'Supplier Registry' exists
    execution_log.append(f"Step 2: Listing tables in base '{supply_chain_base_id}'")
    raw2 = call_tool('airtable_list_tables', {'base_id': supply_chain_base_id})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}")
    tables_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f"Tables result: {str(tables_data)[:300]}... [truncated]")

    # Step 3: List all records in 'Supplier Registry' — this produces a large noisy output
    execution_log.append("Step 3: Listing all records in 'Supplier Registry' (noisy output with many suppliers)")
    raw3 = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': supply_chain_base_id, 'table_name': 'Supplier Registry'})
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}")
    records_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f"Records sample: {str(records_data)[:500]}... [truncated]")

    # Extract records list
    records_list = records_data.get('records', records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else [])
    execution_log.append(f"Total records returned: {len(records_list)}")

    # Step 4: Filter by RiskLevel == 'High' and find lowest QualityScore
    execution_log.append("Step 4: Filtering suppliers with RiskLevel='High' and finding lowest QualityScore")
    high_risk_suppliers = []
    for record in records_list:
        fields = record.get('fields', record)
        risk_level = fields.get('RiskLevel', '')
        if str(risk_level).strip().lower() == 'high':
            high_risk_suppliers.append(record)

    execution_log.append(f"High-risk suppliers count: {len(high_risk_suppliers)}")
    execution_log.append(f"High-risk suppliers sample: {str(high_risk_suppliers[:2])[:400]}")

    # Find the one with the lowest QualityScore
    lowest_quality_record = None
    lowest_quality_score = float('inf')
    for record in high_risk_suppliers:
        fields = record.get('fields', record)
        try:
            score = float(fields.get('QualityScore', float('inf')))
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            score = float('inf')
        if score < lowest_quality_score:
            lowest_quality_score = score
            lowest_quality_record = record

    if not lowest_quality_record:
        execution_log.append("ERROR: No high-risk suppliers found.")
        return "\n".join(execution_log)

    # Extract the SupplierID (record ID) of the worst supplier
    target_record_id = lowest_quality_record.get('id', lowest_quality_record.get('record_id', None))
    target_fields = lowest_quality_record.get('fields', lowest_quality_record)
    target_supplier_id = target_fields.get('SupplierID', target_record_id)
    execution_log.append(f"Lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers: {lowest_quality_score}")
    execution_log.append(f"Target SupplierID: {target_supplier_id}, Record ID: {target_record_id}")

    # Step 5: Fetch the full record details using airtable_get_record
    execution_log.append(f"Step 5: Fetching full record details for record_id='{target_record_id}'")
    raw5 = call_tool('airtable_get_record', {
        'base_id': supply_chain_base_id,
        'table_name': 'Supplier Registry',
        'record_id': target_record_id
    })
    execution_log.append(f"[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}")
    full_record = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f"Full record: {str(full_record)[:500]}")

    # Extract the required fields from the full record
    full_fields = full_record.get('fields', full_record)
    supplier_name = full_fields.get('SupplierName', 'N/A')
    country = full_fields.get('Country', 'N/A')
    annual_value = full_fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 'N/A')
    delivery_on_time = full_fields.get('DeliveryOnTime_Pct', 'N/A')

    execution_log.append("=== FINAL ANSWER ===")
    execution_log.append(f"Supplier with lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers:")
    execution_log.append(f"  SupplierName: {supplier_name}")
    execution_log.append(f"  Country: {country}")
    execution_log.append(f"  AnnualValue_USD: {annual_value}")
    execution_log.append(f"  DeliveryOnTime_Pct: {delivery_on_time}")
    execution_log.append(f"  (QualityScore was: {lowest_quality_score})")

    return "\n".join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: airtable_list_records returns all suppliers across many countries, categories, and tiers; agent must scan every record, filter only RiskLevel='High', then identify the minimum QualityScore among those — all other records are noise

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'; output contains multiple bases with IDs, names, and metadata — mostly irrelevant noise
claims.output_characteristics: List of all accessible Airtable bases; agent must identify the one named 'Supply Chain Analytics' and extract its base_id

claims.description: Call airtable_list_tables using the discovered base_id to confirm 'Supplier Registry' table exists
claims.output_characteristics: List of table names and metadata for the base; contains multiple tables including irrelevant ones

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records to retrieve all records in 'Supplier Registry'; produces large noisy output of many suppliers with all fields
claims.output_characteristics: 50-100+ supplier records each containing SupplierID, SupplierName, Category, Country, AnnualValue_USD, PaymentTermsDays, QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, DefectRate_Pct, TierLevel, CertISO, RiskLevel — most irrelevant; agent must filter RiskLevel='High' and find minimum QualityScore, then remember that record's ID

claims.description: Agent filters records from step 3 output: keeps only those with RiskLevel='High', then identifies the record with the minimum QualityScore and notes its record ID (the SupplierID signal to remember)
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter records where RiskLevel == 'High', then select the record with the lowest QualityScore value
claims.signal_to_remember: record_id of the supplier with lowest QualityScore among High-risk suppliers

claims.description: Call airtable_get_record using the record_id remembered from step 4 to retrieve full details; extract SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct and report them
claims.output_characteristics: Single full record with all fields; agent reads SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct

_design_rationale: 1. Chain is long (4-5 tool calls) because the agent must first discover the base_id dynamically, then list tables to confirm structure, then retrieve all records, and finally fetch the targeted record — no shortcut exists. 2. Noise comes from airtable_list_records returning all suppliers across all risk levels, tiers, categories, and countries; the agent must read through every record and apply two filters (RiskLevel='High', then minimum QualityScore). 3. The signal to extract is the Airtable record ID of the supplier that survives both filters — it is buried inside a list of 50-100+ records, each with 12+ fields. 4. The signal (record ID) from step 3-4 is required as a direct parameter in step 5's airtable_get_record call — if the agent picks the wrong record ID (e.g., confuses it with the highest score, or misses the RiskLevel filter), it will fetch the wrong supplier's details. 5. Losing the signal causes total failure: the final answer (SupplierName, Country, AnnualValue_USD, DeliveryOnTime_Pct) will belong to the wrong supplier. 6. This is Easy difficulty: the chain is linear (A→B→C→D→E), the filtering logic is straightforward (one field equality check + min over one numeric field), and only 1-2 specific values need to be tracked.

task: I need a comprehensive research report on recent transformer-based large language model papers. Please do the following: (1) Search arXiv for papers on 'transformer large language model scaling laws' published between 2023-01-01 and 2024-06-30, retrieving up to 50 results. From those results, identify the paper with the highest number of authors (if tied, pick the one appearing first). Remember its arXiv paper ID. (2) Also search arXiv for 'instruction tuning fine-tuning language model alignment' in the same date range, up to 50 results. From those results, find the paper whose title contains the word 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement' (case-insensitive); if multiple match, pick the most recently submitted one. Remember its arXiv paper ID too. (3) Search PubMed for 'large language model clinical NLP' with 30 results, and from those identify the paper published in the journal 'Nature' or 'npj' (whichever appears first in the list). Remember its PMID. (4) Download and read the full content of the two arXiv papers identified in steps 1 and 2. (5) From the first arXiv paper's full text, extract the exact string that appears as the first section heading after the abstract (i.e., the title of Section 1 or Introduction). From the second arXiv paper's full text, extract the name of the first dataset mentioned (the first proper noun following the word 'dataset' or 'benchmark' in the text). (6) Search your Gmail inbox for emails with the query 'arxiv OR research paper OR LLM' with maxResults=20 and find the most recent email's subject line. (7) Write a structured report to the file /data/llm_research_report.md containing: the two arXiv paper IDs found in steps 1-2, the PMID from step 3, the section heading extracted from the first paper, the dataset name extracted from the second paper, and the most recent email subject from step 6. The report must have a header '# LLM Research Report' and separate sections for each piece of information.

state_assertions.description: The report file /data/llm_research_report.md was created
Report file is non-empty (at least 200 bytes)
Report contains the required header '# LLM Research Report'
Report contains at least one arXiv paper ID pattern (e.g., 2301.XXXXX or 2312.XXXXX)
Report contains a PMID reference (a numeric ID from PubMed)
state_assertions.code: re.search(r'(?i)pmid[:\s]+[0-9]{6,9}', open('/data/llm_research_report.md').read()) is not None or re.search(r'\b[0-9]{7,9}\b', open('/data/llm_research_report.md').read()) is not None

state_assertions.description: Report contains separate sections (multiple ## or ### headings)
Report mentions a section heading or dataset name extracted from paper content

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Each arXiv search returns up to 50 papers with full metadata (title, authors, abstract, dates, categories); PubMed search returns 30 records with mixed journal names; Gmail search returns 20 emails with full headers and previews; full paper text is thousands of lines of markdown. Agent must filter by author count, keyword presence, journal name, and date across all these large outputs and carry specific IDs and extracted strings through to the final write step.

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'transformer large language model scaling laws' (2023-01-01 to 2024-06-30, max 50). Output: ~50 papers each with title, authors list, abstract, IDs, dates. Must find paper with MOST authors and remember its arXiv ID.
claims.output_characteristics: 50 paper records, each with multi-field metadata including variable-length author arrays; most papers have 3-15 authors creating noise; correct paper has maximum author count

claims.description: Extract from step 1 output: identify paper with highest author count (count authors array length for each paper). Remember its arXiv paper ID as signal_A.
claims.extraction_criteria: Count authors for all 50 papers; select paper with maximum count; extract its arXiv ID field
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of paper with most authors — signal_A

claims.description: Search arXiv for 'instruction tuning fine-tuning language model alignment' (2023-01-01 to 2024-06-30, max 50). Output: ~50 more papers. Must find paper whose title contains 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement' (case-insensitive); if multiple, pick most recent. Remember its arXiv ID as signal_B.
claims.output_characteristics: Another 50 paper records with titles, many containing related but non-matching keywords like 'alignment', 'RLHF-free', 'feedback'; only 1-3 match the exact title criterion

claims.description: Extract from step 3 output: filter papers whose title contains 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement'; among matches pick most recently submitted; extract its arXiv ID as signal_B.
claims.extraction_criteria: Title substring match (case-insensitive) for 'RLHF' or 'reinforcement'; sort by submission date descending; extract arXiv ID of first match
claims.signal_to_remember: arXiv ID of matching most-recent paper — signal_B

claims.description: Search PubMed for 'large language model clinical NLP' with 30 results. Output: 30 articles with PMIDs, titles, journals, authors, dates. Find article published in 'Nature' or 'npj' journal; take first occurrence in result list. Remember its PMID as signal_C.
claims.output_characteristics: 30 PubMed records each with title, abstract, journal name, authors, publication date; journals are varied (JAMIA, BMC Medical Informatics, JMIR, Bioinformatics, etc.); must scan journal field for 'Nature' or 'npj' substring

claims.description: Download the arXiv paper identified as signal_A (most authors paper).
Download the arXiv paper identified as signal_B (RLHF/reinforcement paper).

Read full content of signal_A paper. Extract the first section heading after the abstract (title of Section 1 / Introduction). The paper content is thousands of lines; must scan for first '## ' or '# 1' or '**1.' pattern after abstract block. Remember this as signal_D.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: thousands of lines including abstract, introduction, methodology, experiments, references — must extract first post-abstract heading

claims.description: Read full content of signal_B paper. Scan for first occurrence of 'dataset' or 'benchmark' and extract the proper noun (dataset name) immediately following. Remember as signal_E.
claims.output_characteristics: Full paper markdown: thousands of lines with many dataset mentions; must find the FIRST occurrence and extract the correct dataset name

claims.description: Search Gmail for emails matching 'arxiv OR research paper OR LLM' with maxResults=20. Output: up to 20 email records with subjects, senders, dates. Identify most recent email by date. Extract its subject line as signal_F.
claims.output_characteristics: 20 emails with full headers, preview snippets, timestamps; must sort by date and extract subject of the most recent one

claims.description: Write the final report to /data/llm_research_report.md using all extracted signals: signal_A (arXiv ID, most-authors paper), signal_B (arXiv ID, RLHF paper), signal_C (PMID), signal_D (first section heading from paper A), signal_E (first dataset name from paper B), signal_F (most recent email subject). Report must have '# LLM Research Report' header and separate ## sections for each item.

_design_rationale: This task is Hard difficulty because: (1) The chain spans 11 logical steps requiring 9+ distinct tool calls across 4 different servers (arxiv, pubmed, google-workspace, filesystem). (2) Noise is extreme: two arXiv searches each return 50 papers with full metadata (requiring comparison across author arrays and title substring matching), PubMed returns 30 records with varied journal names requiring substring scan, two full paper reads produce thousands of lines each (requiring first-heading and first-dataset extraction), and Gmail returns 20 emails requiring date-sorting. (3) Four distinct signals must be extracted and tracked simultaneously: signal_A (arXiv ID by max-author-count), signal_B (arXiv ID by title keyword + recency), signal_C (PMID by journal name), signal_D and signal_E (text extracted from full paper content), signal_F (email subject by recency). (4) Signals are used many steps after extraction: signal_A from step 1-2 is used in steps 6 and 8; signal_B from step 3-4 is used in steps 7 and 9; all signals converge only at step 11. (5) Losing any signal causes incorrect final report — wrong paper ID means wrong paper is read, wrong section heading or dataset name extracted. (6) The task models a realistic researcher workflow: literature search → paper retrieval → content analysis → email cross-reference → report generation.

task: I'm doing a cross-platform analysis of our oncology drug pipeline and want to validate specific trials against ClinicalTrials.gov data, then compute a geographic accessibility score for the top trial site. Here is what I need: First, look up the 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' base and its 'Oncology Trials' table, then retrieve all records. From those records, identify the single trial that is in 'Phase 3', has Status 'Recruiting', and has the highest-priority sponsor — defined as the sponsor whose name comes first alphabetically among all Phase 3 Recruiting trials. Note the NCTNumber and Country of that trial. Second, use the NCTNumber you found to fetch the full study details from ClinicalTrials.gov, and extract the primary completion date and the lead sponsor name from those details. Third, search ClinicalTrials.gov for all currently RECRUITING Phase 3 trials for condition 'non-small cell lung cancer' and record the total count of results. Fourth, run a trend analysis on ClinicalTrials.gov for 'non-small cell lung cancer' grouped by country, and note the top country (the one with the most studies). Fifth, geocode the Country you extracted from the Airtable record (the country field from the winning Phase 3 Recruiting trial) using OpenStreetMap, and get its coordinates. Finally, use those coordinates to analyze the neighborhood at radius 2000 meters and report: (a) the NCTNumber of the identified trial, (b) the primary completion date from ClinicalTrials.gov, (c) the lead sponsor name from ClinicalTrials.gov, (d) the total count of recruiting Phase 3 NSCLC trials found in the search, (e) the top country from the trend analysis, and (f) the total feature count from the neighborhood analysis of the trial's country capital.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # ── STEP 1: Discover Airtable bases ──────────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to find Oncology Drug Pipeline')
    raw1 = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    bases_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Bases result sample: {str(bases_data)[:400]}')

    # Find the Oncology Drug Pipeline base
    bases_list = bases_data.get('bases', bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else [])
    oncology_base_id = None
    for base in bases_list:
        name = base.get('name', base.get('title', ''))
        if 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' in name:
            oncology_base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', ''))
            break
    execution_log.append(f'Found Oncology Drug Pipeline base_id: {oncology_base_id}')

    # ── STEP 2: List tables in the base ──────────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Listing tables in Oncology Drug Pipeline base')
    raw2 = call_tool('airtable_list_tables', {'base_id': oncology_base_id})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}')
    tables_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    execution_log.append(f'Tables result: {str(tables_data)[:300]}')

    # ── STEP 3: List all records in Oncology Trials table ────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Fetching all records from Oncology Trials table')
    raw3 = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': oncology_base_id, 'table_name': 'Oncology Trials'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    records_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    records = records_data.get('records', records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else [])
    execution_log.append(f'Total records fetched: {len(records)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample record: {str(records[0])[:400] if records else "none"}')

    # ── STEP 4: Filter Phase 3 Recruiting trials, pick alphabetically first sponsor ──
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Filtering Phase 3 Recruiting trials and finding alphabetically first sponsor')
    phase3_recruiting = []
    for rec in records:
        fields = rec.get('fields', rec)
        phase = fields.get('Phase', '')
        status = fields.get('Status', '')
        if 'Phase 3' in str(phase) and 'Recruiting' in str(status):
            phase3_recruiting.append(fields)

    execution_log.append(f'Phase 3 Recruiting trials count: {len(phase3_recruiting)}')
    for t in phase3_recruiting:
        execution_log.append(f"  Sponsor={t.get('Sponsor','?')} NCT={t.get('NCTNumber','?')} Country={t.get('Country','?')}")

    # Sort by Sponsor alphabetically, pick first
    phase3_recruiting.sort(key=lambda x: x.get('Sponsor', '').lower())
    winner = phase3_recruiting[0] if phase3_recruiting else {}
    winning_nct = winner.get('NCTNumber', 'UNKNOWN')
    winning_country = winner.get('Country', 'UNKNOWN')
    winning_sponsor_airtable = winner.get('Sponsor', 'UNKNOWN')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Winning trial: NCT={winning_nct}, Country={winning_country}, Sponsor={winning_sponsor_airtable}')

    # ── STEP 5: Fetch full study from ClinicalTrials.gov ─────────────────────
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Fetching ClinicalTrials.gov study for NCT ID: {winning_nct}')
    raw5 = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_get_study', {'nctIds': winning_nct, 'summaryOnly': False})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    study_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f'Study data sample: {str(study_data)[:600]}')

    # Extract primary completion date and lead sponsor from study
    # ClinicalTrials.gov returns complex nested structure
    studies_list = study_data.get('studies', study_data if isinstance(study_data, list) else [study_data])
    study = studies_list[0] if studies_list else study_data
    protocol = study.get('protocolSection', study)
    status_module = protocol.get('statusModule', {})
    sponsor_module = protocol.get('sponsorCollaboratorsModule', {})
    primary_completion_date = status_module.get('primaryCompletionDateStruct', {}).get('date', status_module.get('primaryCompletionDate', 'N/A'))
    lead_sponsor = sponsor_module.get('leadSponsor', {}).get('name', 'N/A')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Primary completion date: {primary_completion_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Lead sponsor (ClinicalTrials.gov): {lead_sponsor}')

    # ── STEP 6: Search NSCLC recruiting Phase 3 trials ───────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Searching ClinicalTrials.gov for recruiting Phase 3 NSCLC trials')
    raw6 = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_list_studies', {
        'query': {'cond': 'non-small cell lung cancer'},
        'filter': {'overallStatus': ['RECRUITING']},
        'pageSize': 50,
        'countTotal': True
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw6)[:200]}')
    search_data = json.loads(raw6) if isinstance(raw6, str) else raw6
    execution_log.append(f'Search result sample: {str(search_data)[:500]}')
    total_nsclc_count = search_data.get('totalCount', search_data.get('total', len(search_data.get('studies', []))))
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Total recruiting Phase 3 NSCLC trials: {total_nsclc_count}')

    # ── STEP 7: Trend analysis by country for NSCLC ──────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('Step 7: Running trend analysis by country for non-small cell lung cancer')
    raw7 = call_tool('clinicaltrialsgov-mcp-server_clinicaltrials_analyze_trends', {
        'query': {'cond': 'non-small cell lung cancer'},
        'analysisType': 'countByCountry'
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    trend_data = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    execution_log.append(f'Trend data sample: {str(trend_data)[:600]}')

    # Find top country
    country_counts = trend_data.get('results', trend_data.get('data', trend_data if isinstance(trend_data, list) else []))
    top_country = None
    top_count = -1
    if isinstance(country_counts, list):
        for entry in country_counts:
            count_val = entry.get('count', entry.get('value', 0))
            country_name = entry.get('country', entry.get('name', entry.get('label', '')))
            if count_val > top_count:
                top_count = count_val
                top_country = country_name
    elif isinstance(country_counts, dict):
        for k, v in country_counts.items():
            if isinstance(v, (int, float)) and v > top_count:
                top_count = v
                top_country = k
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Top country from trend analysis: {top_country} ({top_count} studies)')

    # ── STEP 8: Geocode the winning trial's country ───────────────────────────
    execution_log.append(f'Step 8: Geocoding country: {winning_country}')
    raw8 = call_tool('osm-mcp-server_geocode_address', {'address': winning_country})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw8)[:200]}')
    geo_data = json.loads(raw8) if isinstance(raw8, str) else raw8
    geo_result = geo_data.get('result', geo_data if isinstance(geo_data, list) else [])
    if isinstance(geo_result, list) and geo_result:
        geo_entry = geo_result[0]
    elif isinstance(geo_result, dict):
        geo_entry = geo_result
    else:
        geo_entry = {}
    country_lat = float(geo_entry.get('lat', 0))
    country_lon = float(geo_entry.get('lon', 0))
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Coordinates for {winning_country}: lat={country_lat}, lon={country_lon}')

    # ── STEP 9: Analyze neighborhood at those coordinates ────────────────────
    execution_log.append(f'Step 9: Analyzing neighborhood at lat={country_lat}, lon={country_lon}, radius=2000')
    raw9 = call_tool('osm-mcp-server_analyze_neighborhood', {
        'latitude': country_lat,
        'longitude': country_lon,
        'radius': 2000
    })
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw9)[:200]}')
    neighborhood_data = json.loads(raw9) if isinstance(raw9, str) else raw9
    nbhd_result = neighborhood_data.get('result', neighborhood_data)
    total_features = nbhd_result.get('total_features', nbhd_result.get('totalFeatures', nbhd_result.get('feature_count', nbhd_result.get('total', 'N/A'))))
    execution_log.append(f'SIGNAL EXTRACTED - Total feature count from neighborhood analysis: {total_features}')
    execution_log.append(f'Neighborhood result sample: {str(nbhd_result)[:500]}')

    # ── FINAL SUMMARY ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL SUMMARY ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(a) NCTNumber of identified Phase 3 Recruiting trial (alphabetically first sponsor): {winning_nct}')
    execution_log.append(f'(b) Primary completion date from ClinicalTrials.gov: {primary_completion_date}')
    execution_log.append(f'(c) Lead sponsor name from ClinicalTrials.gov: {lead_sponsor}')
    execution_log.append(f'(d) Total count of recruiting NSCLC trials found: {total_nsclc_count}')
    execution_log.append(f'(e) Top country from trend analysis: {top_country}')
    execution_log.append(f'(f) Total feature count from neighborhood analysis of {winning_country}: {total_features}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Airtable returns hundreds of oncology trial records with many irrelevant fields (TherapeuticArea, Budget, Phase 1/2 entries, non-Recruiting statuses); ClinicalTrials.gov returns deeply nested JSON with hundreds of fields per study; trend analysis returns all countries with counts; geocode returns multiple candidate locations with verbose OSM metadata; neighborhood analysis returns a large categorized amenity profile

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id of 'Oncology Drug Pipeline' from among all listed bases
claims.output_characteristics: List of all accessible Airtable bases with IDs and names; irrelevant bases (Marketing Analytics Hub, Supply Chain Analytics) included as noise

claims.description: Call airtable_list_tables with the discovered base_id to verify the 'Oncology Trials' table exists
claims.output_characteristics: Table metadata with field definitions; noisy schema information not needed for the task

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records to fetch all records from 'Oncology Trials' — large noisy output with all phases, statuses, regions
claims.output_characteristics: Hundreds of records covering Phase 1, 2, 3, 4 across all statuses (Completed, Terminated, Withdrawn, Not Yet Recruiting, Recruiting); irrelevant records constitute the majority of output

claims.description: Filter records to Phase 3 + Recruiting status only, sort sponsors alphabetically, extract NCTNumber and Country of the alphabetically-first sponsor's trial
claims.extraction_criteria: Phase field contains 'Phase 3' AND Status field is 'Recruiting'; sort by Sponsor alphabetically; take the first entry; record NCTNumber and Country fields
claims.signal_to_remember: NCTNumber (e.g. NCT0XXXXXXX) and Country (e.g. 'United States') of winning trial

claims.description: Call clinicaltrials_get_study with the extracted NCTNumber to retrieve full ClinicalTrials.gov study details
claims.output_characteristics: Very large nested JSON with protocolSection, resultsSection, derivedSection; hundreds of fields including eligibility criteria, arm descriptions, outcome measures — only primaryCompletionDate and leadSponsor name are needed

claims.description: Extract primary completion date and lead sponsor name from the deeply nested ClinicalTrials.gov study JSON
claims.extraction_criteria: Navigate protocolSection.statusModule.primaryCompletionDateStruct.date and protocolSection.sponsorCollaboratorsModule.leadSponsor.name
claims.signal_to_remember: Primary completion date string and lead sponsor name string

claims.description: Call clinicaltrials_list_studies for condition 'non-small cell lung cancer' with RECRUITING filter to get total count
claims.output_characteristics: Large list of studies with full metadata; only totalCount field matters; individual study records are noise

claims.description: Call clinicaltrials_analyze_trends for 'non-small cell lung cancer' grouped by country to find the top country
claims.output_characteristics: Statistical breakdown by country across hundreds of countries; agent must sort/filter to find the single country with the highest count
claims.signal_to_remember: Name of top country by study count

claims.description: Geocode the winning trial's Country (from step 4) using OSM geocode_address to get lat/lon coordinates
claims.output_characteristics: Multiple candidate locations with verbose OSM metadata (bounding boxes, display names, importance scores, type/class fields); only first result's lat/lon is needed

claims.description: Call analyze_neighborhood at the geocoded coordinates with radius=2000 to get total feature count
claims.uses_signal: lat/lon from step 9 (which depends on Country from step 4)
claims.output_characteristics: Comprehensive neighborhood profile with dozens of amenity categories, subcategories, and individual feature entries; only total_features count is needed for the final answer

claims.description: Report all six extracted values: (a) NCTNumber, (b) primary completion date, (c) lead sponsor, (d) NSCLC recruiting count, (e) top country from trends, (f) neighborhood feature count
claims.uses_signal: All signals extracted in steps 4, 6, 7, 8, and 10 must be remembered and reported correctly

_design_rationale: 1) LONG CHAIN: The task requires 9+ tool calls: airtable_list_bases → airtable_list_tables → airtable_list_records → (filter/extract) → clinicaltrials_get_study → (extract from nested JSON) → clinicaltrials_list_studies → clinicaltrials_analyze_trends → osm_geocode_address → osm_analyze_neighborhood. This exceeds the Hard difficulty threshold of 9 tool calls. 2) NOISE SOURCES: Airtable returns all oncology trials across all phases and statuses; the agent must filter to Phase 3+Recruiting only from a large record set. ClinicalTrials.gov study detail JSON is deeply nested with hundreds of irrelevant fields. The trends analysis returns every country. The geocode result returns multiple candidate locations with verbose OSM metadata. The neighborhood analysis returns detailed amenity breakdowns for dozens of categories. 3) CRITICAL SIGNALS: (A) NCTNumber and Country extracted from Airtable in step 4 — NCTNumber is needed in step 5 (ClinicalTrials.gov fetch) and Country is needed in step 9 (geocoding); (B) Primary completion date and lead sponsor extracted from ClinicalTrials.gov in step 6 — needed only in the final report; (C) NSCLC total count from step 7; (D) Top country from trend analysis in step 8; (E) Coordinates from geocoding in step 9 — immediately needed in step 10. 4) MEMORY DEPENDENCY: The Country from Airtable (step 4) must be remembered all the way through steps 5-8 until step 9 (geocoding). If the agent forgets or confuses which Country was extracted, the geocoding and neighborhood analysis will be for the wrong location, making items (a) and (f) wrong. The NCTNumber is used immediately in step 5 but must be carried through to the final report. 5) FAILURE MODE: Any confusion between multiple Phase 3 Recruiting trials (wrong sponsor alphabetical selection) propagates errors through NCT fetch, geocoding, and neighborhood analysis — all five remaining signals depend on the correct winner being identified. 6) DIFFICULTY JUSTIFICATION: Hard level is achieved by (a) 3 distinct external servers (Airtable, ClinicalTrials.gov, OpenStreetMap), (b) 9+ tool calls, (c) 4+ distinct signals to track simultaneously, and (d) the alphabetical-sponsor-sort filter criterion being a non-trivial extraction rule buried in a large record set.

task: I'm researching the Metropolitan Museum of Art's ancient Egyptian collection. First, search for all museum objects in the Egyptian Art department that match the query 'scarab'. From the returned list of object IDs, retrieve the details of the first object ID in the list. Note the exact title and the artistDisplayName of that first object. Then search Wikipedia for the article titled 'Scarab (artifact)' and get its full content. Finally, look up the Wikipedia article titled 'Egyptian Museum' and extract its key facts. Report back: (1) the Met object ID, title, and artistDisplayName of the first scarab object found, (2) the number of characters in the Wikipedia 'Scarab (artifact)' article content, and (3) five key facts from the 'Egyptian Museum' Wikipedia article.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List departments to find Egyptian Art department ID
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Met Museum departments to find Egyptian Art department ID')
    raw1 = call_tool('met-museum_list-departments', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    depts_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Departments result sample: {str(depts_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Egyptian Art department ID
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Extracting Egyptian Art department ID from departments list')
    departments = []
    if isinstance(depts_data, dict):
        departments = depts_data.get('departments', depts_data.get('data', []))
    elif isinstance(depts_data, list):
        departments = depts_data
    egyptian_dept_id = None
    for dept in departments:
        name = dept.get('displayName', dept.get('name', ''))
        if 'egypt' in name.lower():
            egyptian_dept_id = dept.get('departmentId', dept.get('id', None))
            execution_log.append(f'Found Egyptian Art department: name={name}, id={egyptian_dept_id}')
            break
    if egyptian_dept_id is None:
        execution_log.append('WARNING: Could not find Egyptian Art department, will search without departmentId')

    # Step 3: Search for scarab objects in Egyptian Art department
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Searching Met Museum for scarab objects in Egyptian Art department')
    search_params = {'q': 'scarab', 'hasImages': False}
    if egyptian_dept_id is not None:
        search_params['departmentId'] = egyptian_dept_id
    raw3 = call_tool('met-museum_search-museum-objects', search_params)
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    search_data = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f'Search result sample: {str(search_data)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract first object ID from the large list
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Extracting first object ID from search results (signal to remember)')
    object_ids = []
    if isinstance(search_data, dict):
        object_ids = search_data.get('objectIDs', search_data.get('data', []))
    elif isinstance(search_data, list):
        object_ids = search_data
    first_object_id = object_ids[0] if object_ids else None
    total_count = search_data.get('total', len(object_ids)) if isinstance(search_data, dict) else len(object_ids)
    execution_log.append(f'Total objects found: {total_count}')
    execution_log.append(f'First object ID (SIGNAL TO REMEMBER): {first_object_id}')

    # Step 5: Get details of first object
    execution_log.append(f'Step 5: Getting details for first object ID {first_object_id}')
    raw5 = call_tool('met-museum_get-museum-object', {'objectId': first_object_id, 'returnImage': False})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    obj_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f'Object details sample: {str(obj_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract title and artistDisplayName
    obj_title = obj_data.get('title', 'Unknown')
    artist_name = obj_data.get('artistDisplayName', 'Unknown')
    execution_log.append(f'Object ID: {first_object_id}')
    execution_log.append(f'Object Title (SIGNAL): {obj_title}')
    execution_log.append(f'Artist Display Name (SIGNAL): {artist_name}')

    # Step 6: Get Wikipedia article for Scarab (artifact)
    execution_log.append("Step 6: Getting Wikipedia full article for 'Scarab (artifact)'")
    raw6 = call_tool('wikipedia_get_article', {'title': 'Scarab (artifact)'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw6)[:200]}')
    wiki_scarab = json.loads(raw6) if isinstance(raw6, str) else raw6
    execution_log.append(f'Scarab article sample: {str(wiki_scarab)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Count characters in article content
    scarab_content = ''
    if isinstance(wiki_scarab, dict):
        scarab_content = wiki_scarab.get('content', wiki_scarab.get('text', wiki_scarab.get('extract', '')))
    elif isinstance(wiki_scarab, str):
        scarab_content = wiki_scarab
    scarab_char_count = len(scarab_content)
    execution_log.append(f'Scarab article character count: {scarab_char_count}')

    # Step 7: Extract key facts from Egyptian Museum Wikipedia article
    execution_log.append("Step 7: Extracting key facts from Wikipedia article 'Egyptian Museum'")
    raw7 = call_tool('wikipedia_extract_key_facts', {'title': 'Egyptian Museum', 'count': 5})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    key_facts_data = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    execution_log.append(f'Key facts sample: {str(key_facts_data)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    key_facts = []
    if isinstance(key_facts_data, dict):
        key_facts = key_facts_data.get('facts', key_facts_data.get('key_facts', []))
    elif isinstance(key_facts_data, list):
        key_facts = key_facts_data
    execution_log.append(f'Key facts extracted: {key_facts}')

    # Final summary
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER SUMMARY ===')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) First Scarab object in Met Egyptian Art dept:')
    execution_log.append(f'    Object ID: {first_object_id}')
    execution_log.append(f'    Title: {obj_title}')
    execution_log.append(f'    Artist Display Name: {artist_name}')
    execution_log.append(f"(2) Wikipedia 'Scarab (artifact)' article character count: {scarab_char_count}")
    execution_log.append(f"(3) Five key facts from 'Egyptian Museum' Wikipedia article:")
    for i, fact in enumerate(key_facts[:5], 1):
        execution_log.append(f'    Fact {i}: {fact}')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: Search returns hundreds of object IDs (large list); department listing returns all 19 departments with irrelevant info; object details include dozens of fields (culture, period, medium, dimensions, provenance, etc.); Wikipedia article contains thousands of characters of text with many unrelated sections

claims.description: List all Met Museum departments to identify the Egyptian Art department ID
claims.output_characteristics: Returns all 19 departments with IDs and display names — noisy because most departments are irrelevant (Greek/Roman, Asian Art, Modern Art, etc.)

claims.description: Extract the Egyptian Art department ID from the noisy departments list
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter departments by name containing 'Egypt', extract its departmentId integer

claims.description: Search Met Museum for scarab objects, using the extracted Egyptian Art department ID
claims.output_characteristics: Returns hundreds of object IDs matching 'scarab' — large noisy list where only the first ID matters

claims.description: Extract the first object ID from the large list of search results
claims.extraction_criteria: Take objectIDs[0] from search results
claims.signal_to_remember: First object ID, plus the object's title and artistDisplayName retrieved in next step

claims.description: Get full details of the first scarab object using the remembered object ID
claims.output_characteristics: Returns dozens of fields (culture, period, medium, dimensions, provenance, URLs, etc.) — must extract only title and artistDisplayName

claims.description: Get the full Wikipedia article for 'Scarab (artifact)' and count its characters
claims.output_characteristics: Returns thousands of characters of article text across many sections — agent must count characters of full content field

claims.description: Extract 5 key facts from the Wikipedia 'Egyptian Museum' article
claims.output_characteristics: Returns structured list of facts extracted from a long Wikipedia article about the Egyptian Museum in Cairo

_design_rationale: (1) Why chain is long: The task requires 5+ tool calls — listing departments, searching objects, fetching object details, getting a Wikipedia article, and extracting key facts — each building on the previous step. (2) Where noise comes from: The departments list has 19 entries; the scarab search returns hundreds of object IDs; the object detail record has 30+ fields; the Wikipedia article has thousands of characters across many sections. (3) What signal must be extracted: The first object ID from the scarab search results (hundreds of IDs), then from its details the title and artistDisplayName (buried among 30+ fields). (4) How signal is used later: The object ID from step 3 is immediately used in step 4 (get-museum-object); the title and artistDisplayName from step 4 are reported in the final answer. (5) Why losing signal causes failure: If the agent picks the wrong object ID or misreads the title/artistDisplayName, the final answer about the Met object is wrong. Similarly, miscounting Wikipedia article characters or extracting wrong facts fails the task. (6) Why this matches Easy difficulty: Linear chain of 5 tool calls, 2 pieces of information to extract and track, moderate noise volume, straightforward filtering (first item in list, specific named fields).

task: I'm doing a supplier risk review for our procurement team. In the 'Supply Chain Analytics' Airtable base, list all records from the 'Supplier Registry' table, then identify the supplier with the highest QualityScore among those with RiskLevel equal to 'High'. Once you have that supplier's name, search for that specific supplier by name in the same table to retrieve their full record details. Finally, calculate what percentage of their AnnualValue_USD represents relative to a benchmark of $5,000,000 (i.e., AnnualValue_USD / 5000000 * 100), and report the supplier's name, their QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, and that calculated percentage rounded to 2 decimal places.

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: List all Airtable bases to find the Supply Chain Analytics base
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Listing all Airtable bases to discover base_id')
    raw1 = call_tool('airtable_list_bases', {})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    bases_data = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Bases result sample: {str(bases_data)[:400]}')

    # Extract the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'
    base_id = None
    bases_list = bases_data if isinstance(bases_data, list) else bases_data.get('bases', [])
    for base in bases_list:
        base_name = base.get('name', '')
        execution_log.append(f'  Found base: {base_name}')
        if 'Supply Chain Analytics' in base_name:
            base_id = base.get('id', base.get('base_id', None))
            execution_log.append(f'  --> Matched! base_id = {base_id}')
            break
    if not base_id:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: Could not find Supply Chain Analytics base')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    # Step 2: List all records in the Supplier Registry table
    execution_log.append(f'Step 2: Listing all records in Supplier Registry (base_id={base_id})')
    raw2 = call_tool('airtable_list_records', {'base_id': base_id, 'table_name': 'Supplier Registry'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw2)[:200]}')
    records_data = json.loads(raw2) if isinstance(raw2, str) else raw2
    records = records_data if isinstance(records_data, list) else records_data.get('records', [])
    execution_log.append(f'Total records returned: {len(records)}')
    execution_log.append(f'Sample records (first 2): {str(records[:2])[:600]}')

    # Step 3: Filter records where RiskLevel == 'High' and find highest QualityScore
    execution_log.append('Step 3: Filtering records with RiskLevel=High and finding max QualityScore')
    high_risk_suppliers = []
    for rec in records:
        fields = rec.get('fields', rec)
        risk_level = fields.get('RiskLevel', '')
        if risk_level == 'High':
            high_risk_suppliers.append(rec)
            execution_log.append(f"  High-risk supplier found: {fields.get('SupplierName', 'N/A')} QualityScore={fields.get('QualityScore', 'N/A')}")

    execution_log.append(f'Total High-risk suppliers: {len(high_risk_suppliers)}')

    if not high_risk_suppliers:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: No High-risk suppliers found')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)

    best_supplier = None
    best_quality = -1
    for rec in high_risk_suppliers:
        fields = rec.get('fields', rec)
        quality = fields.get('QualityScore', 0)
        try:
            quality_val = float(quality)
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            quality_val = 0
        if quality_val > best_quality:
            best_quality = quality_val
            best_supplier = rec

    best_fields = best_supplier.get('fields', best_supplier)
    best_name = best_fields.get('SupplierName', 'N/A')
    execution_log.append(f'Signal extracted: Best High-risk supplier = "{best_name}" with QualityScore={best_quality}')

    # Step 4: Search for that specific supplier by name
    execution_log.append(f'Step 4: Searching for supplier "{best_name}" in Supplier Registry')
    raw4 = call_tool('airtable_search_records', {'base_id': base_id, 'table_name': 'Supplier Registry', 'field_name': 'SupplierName', 'value': best_name})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw4)[:200]}')
    search_data = json.loads(raw4) if isinstance(raw4, str) else raw4
    search_records = search_data if isinstance(search_data, list) else search_data.get('records', [])
    execution_log.append(f'Search returned {len(search_records)} record(s)')
    execution_log.append(f'Full record details: {str(search_records)[:600]}')

    if search_records:
        found_rec = search_records[0]
        found_fields = found_rec.get('fields', found_rec)
        annual_value = found_fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 0)
        delivery_pct = found_fields.get('DeliveryOnTime_Pct', 'N/A')
        quality_score = found_fields.get('QualityScore', best_quality)
        execution_log.append(f'Retrieved: AnnualValue_USD={annual_value}, DeliveryOnTime_Pct={delivery_pct}, QualityScore={quality_score}')
    else:
        annual_value = best_fields.get('AnnualValue_USD', 0)
        delivery_pct = best_fields.get('DeliveryOnTime_Pct', 'N/A')
        quality_score = best_quality
        execution_log.append(f'Using data from listing step: AnnualValue_USD={annual_value}, DeliveryOnTime_Pct={delivery_pct}')

    # Step 5: Calculate percentage relative to $5,000,000 benchmark
    execution_log.append('Step 5: Calculating AnnualValue_USD / 5000000 * 100')
    try:
        annual_val_num = float(annual_value)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        annual_val_num = 0
    expression = f'{annual_val_num} / 5000000 * 100'
    raw5 = call_tool('calculator_calculate', {'expression': expression})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    calc_data = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    calc_result = calc_data.get('result', str(raw5))
    try:
        pct_rounded = round(float(calc_result), 2)
    except (TypeError, ValueError):
        pct_rounded = 'N/A'
    execution_log.append(f'Calculated percentage: {pct_rounded}%')

    # Final answer
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL ANSWER ===')
    execution_log.append(f'Supplier Name: {best_name}')
    execution_log.append(f'QualityScore: {quality_score}')
    execution_log.append(f'DeliveryOnTime_Pct: {delivery_pct}')
    execution_log.append(f'AnnualValue as % of $5M benchmark: {pct_rounded}%')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: airtable_list_records returns all supplier records across multiple countries, tiers, and risk levels; agent must filter only RiskLevel='High' records (ignoring Medium/Low), then find the max QualityScore among those—burying the target name in a large list with many irrelevant fields.

claims.description: Call airtable_list_bases to discover the base_id for 'Supply Chain Analytics'—returns a noisy list of all accessible bases with IDs, names, and metadata.
claims.output_characteristics: List of bases with IDs and names; agent must identify the correct base by matching 'Supply Chain Analytics'

claims.description: Call airtable_list_records on 'Supplier Registry' using the discovered base_id—returns all supplier records with many fields (SupplierID, SupplierName, Category, Country, AnnualValue_USD, PaymentTermsDays, QualityScore, DeliveryOnTime_Pct, DefectRate_Pct, TierLevel, CertISO, RiskLevel), most of which are irrelevant noise.
claims.output_characteristics: 50-100+ records, each with 12+ fields; most records have RiskLevel != 'High', making them noise; agent must filter and find max QualityScore among High-risk only

claims.description: Filter records where RiskLevel='High', identify the one with the highest QualityScore, and extract their SupplierName as the signal to remember.
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter by RiskLevel == 'High', then find max QualityScore among filtered records, extract SupplierName
claims.signal_to_remember: SupplierName of the High-risk supplier with the highest QualityScore

claims.description: Call airtable_search_records using the remembered SupplierName to retrieve the full detailed record—verifying and enriching the data with exact field values including AnnualValue_USD and DeliveryOnTime_Pct.
Call calculator_calculate with the expression AnnualValue_USD / 5000000 * 100 using the value retrieved in step 4, and report the final answer with all required fields.
claims.uses_signal: AnnualValue_USD from the identified supplier's record

_design_rationale: (1) Why chain is long: The agent must first discover the base ID (step 1), then list all records (step 2), then filter and identify the target supplier (step 3), then search to confirm full details (step 4), then calculate the percentage (step 5)—5 distinct tool calls forming a natural procurement analysis workflow. (2) Where noise comes from: airtable_list_records returns every supplier regardless of RiskLevel; the agent sees Medium, Low, and High risk suppliers mixed together, each with 12+ fields—only a few High-risk records and one specific field (QualityScore) matter. (3) What signal must be extracted: The SupplierName of the High-risk supplier with the maximum QualityScore, buried among many irrelevant records. (4) How signal is used later: The extracted SupplierName is passed directly to airtable_search_records in step 4; if the wrong name is remembered, the wrong record is fetched and the final calculation is wrong. (5) Why losing signal causes failure: Passing the wrong supplier name to search returns different AnnualValue_USD/DeliveryOnTime_Pct values, making every part of the final answer incorrect. (6) Why this matches Easy difficulty: Linear 5-step chain, single signal to extract (one name), moderate noise (50-100 records), and clear filtering criteria (RiskLevel='High' + max QualityScore).

task: I'm planning a road trip to visit two famous national parks and want to compare their accessibility and current conditions. First, search for national parks in Utah (state code 'UT') and find the park whose name contains 'Zion' — note its park code. Then get the full details for that park and note its exact GPS entrance coordinates (latitude and longitude). Next, search for national parks in Arizona (state code 'AZ') and find the park whose name contains 'Grand Canyon' — note its park code. Get the full details for that Grand Canyon park and note its GPS entrance coordinates. Now use the GPS coordinates of the Zion park entrance as the origin and the GPS coordinates of the Grand Canyon entrance as the destination to get driving directions between them. Finally, check for any current alerts at BOTH parks (using their park codes) and report: (1) the driving distance and estimated travel time between the two park entrances, (2) how many total active alerts exist across both parks combined, and (3) the title of the first alert listed for each park (or 'No alerts' if none).

ground_truth.dynamic_reference_script:
def generate_reference_answer():
    import json
    from mcp_client import call_tool

    execution_log = []

    # Step 1: Find parks in Utah
    execution_log.append('Step 1: Searching for national parks in Utah (stateCode=UT)')
    raw1 = call_tool('national-parks_findParks', {'stateCode': 'UT', 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw1)[:200]}')
    data1 = json.loads(raw1) if isinstance(raw1, str) else raw1
    execution_log.append(f'Utah parks result sample: {str(data1)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Zion park code and coordinates
    execution_log.append('Step 2: Extracting Zion park code from Utah results...')
    ut_parks = data1.get('data', data1 if isinstance(data1, list) else [])
    zion_park = None
    for park in ut_parks:
        name = park.get('fullName', '') or park.get('name', '')
        if 'Zion' in name:
            zion_park = park
            break
    if zion_park is None:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: Zion park not found in Utah results')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)
    zion_code = zion_park.get('parkCode', 'zion')
    execution_log.append(f'Found Zion park code: {zion_code}')

    # Step 3: Get full details for Zion
    execution_log.append(f'Step 3: Getting full details for Zion (parkCode={zion_code})')
    raw3 = call_tool('national-parks_getParkDetails', {'parkCode': zion_code})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw3)[:200]}')
    data3 = json.loads(raw3) if isinstance(raw3, str) else raw3
    execution_log.append(f'Zion details sample: {str(data3)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Zion GPS coordinates
    execution_log.append('Step 4: Extracting Zion GPS entrance coordinates...')
    zion_detail = data3.get('data', data3) if isinstance(data3, dict) else data3
    if isinstance(zion_detail, list):
        zion_detail = zion_detail[0] if zion_detail else {}
    zion_lat = zion_detail.get('latitude', None) or zion_detail.get('latLong', '')
    zion_lon = zion_detail.get('longitude', None)
    # Try parsing latLong string if separate fields not available
    if zion_lat and ',' in str(zion_lat) and not zion_lon:
        parts = str(zion_lat).replace('lat:', '').replace('long:', '').split(',')
        zion_lat = parts[0].strip()
        zion_lon = parts[1].strip() if len(parts) > 1 else None
    execution_log.append(f'Zion coordinates: lat={zion_lat}, lon={zion_lon}')

    # Step 5: Find parks in Arizona
    execution_log.append('Step 5: Searching for national parks in Arizona (stateCode=AZ)')
    raw5 = call_tool('national-parks_findParks', {'stateCode': 'AZ', 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw5)[:200]}')
    data5 = json.loads(raw5) if isinstance(raw5, str) else raw5
    execution_log.append(f'Arizona parks result sample: {str(data5)[:400]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Grand Canyon park code
    execution_log.append('Step 6: Extracting Grand Canyon park code from Arizona results...')
    az_parks = data5.get('data', data5 if isinstance(data5, list) else [])
    gc_park = None
    for park in az_parks:
        name = park.get('fullName', '') or park.get('name', '')
        if 'Grand Canyon' in name:
            gc_park = park
            break
    if gc_park is None:
        execution_log.append('ERROR: Grand Canyon park not found in Arizona results')
        return '\n'.join(execution_log)
    gc_code = gc_park.get('parkCode', 'grca')
    execution_log.append(f'Found Grand Canyon park code: {gc_code}')

    # Step 7: Get full details for Grand Canyon
    execution_log.append(f'Step 7: Getting full details for Grand Canyon (parkCode={gc_code})')
    raw7 = call_tool('national-parks_getParkDetails', {'parkCode': gc_code})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw7)[:200]}')
    data7 = json.loads(raw7) if isinstance(raw7, str) else raw7
    execution_log.append(f'Grand Canyon details sample: {str(data7)[:500]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract Grand Canyon GPS coordinates
    execution_log.append('Step 8: Extracting Grand Canyon GPS entrance coordinates...')
    gc_detail = data7.get('data', data7) if isinstance(data7, dict) else data7
    if isinstance(gc_detail, list):
        gc_detail = gc_detail[0] if gc_detail else {}
    gc_lat = gc_detail.get('latitude', None) or gc_detail.get('latLong', '')
    gc_lon = gc_detail.get('longitude', None)
    if gc_lat and ',' in str(gc_lat) and not gc_lon:
        parts = str(gc_lat).replace('lat:', '').replace('long:', '').split(',')
        gc_lat = parts[0].strip()
        gc_lon = parts[1].strip() if len(parts) > 1 else None
    execution_log.append(f'Grand Canyon coordinates: lat={gc_lat}, lon={gc_lon}')

    # Step 9: Get driving directions from Zion to Grand Canyon using extracted coordinates
    execution_log.append(f'Step 9: Getting driving directions from Zion ({zion_lat},{zion_lon}) to Grand Canyon ({gc_lat},{gc_lon})')
    origin_str = f'{zion_lat},{zion_lon}'
    dest_str = f'{gc_lat},{gc_lon}'
    raw9 = call_tool('google-maps_maps_directions', {'origin': origin_str, 'destination': dest_str, 'mode': 'driving'})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw9)[:200]}')
    data9 = json.loads(raw9) if isinstance(raw9, str) else raw9
    execution_log.append(f'Directions result sample: {str(data9)[:600]}... [truncated]')

    # Extract distance and duration
    execution_log.append('Extracting distance and travel time from directions...')
    routes = data9.get('routes', [])
    distance_text = 'unknown'
    duration_text = 'unknown'
    if routes:
        legs = routes[0].get('legs', [])
        if legs:
            distance_text = legs[0].get('distance', {}).get('text', 'unknown')
            duration_text = legs[0].get('duration', {}).get('text', 'unknown')
    execution_log.append(f'Driving distance: {distance_text}, Estimated time: {duration_text}')

    # Step 10: Get alerts for Zion
    execution_log.append(f'Step 10: Getting alerts for Zion (parkCode={zion_code})')
    raw10 = call_tool('national-parks_getAlerts', {'parkCode': zion_code, 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw10)[:200]}')
    data10 = json.loads(raw10) if isinstance(raw10, str) else raw10
    execution_log.append(f'Zion alerts sample: {str(data10)[:400]}... [truncated]')
    zion_alerts = data10.get('data', data10 if isinstance(data10, list) else [])
    zion_alert_count = len(zion_alerts)
    zion_first_alert_title = zion_alerts[0].get('title', 'No title') if zion_alerts else 'No alerts'
    execution_log.append(f'Zion alert count: {zion_alert_count}, First alert title: {zion_first_alert_title}')

    # Step 11: Get alerts for Grand Canyon
    execution_log.append(f'Step 11: Getting alerts for Grand Canyon (parkCode={gc_code})')
    raw11 = call_tool('national-parks_getAlerts', {'parkCode': gc_code, 'limit': 50})
    execution_log.append(f'[RAW] {str(raw11)[:200]}')
    data11 = json.loads(raw11) if isinstance(raw11, str) else raw11
    execution_log.append(f'Grand Canyon alerts sample: {str(data11)[:400]}... [truncated]')
    gc_alerts = data11.get('data', data11 if isinstance(data11, list) else [])
    gc_alert_count = len(gc_alerts)
    gc_first_alert_title = gc_alerts[0].get('title', 'No title') if gc_alerts else 'No alerts'
    execution_log.append(f'Grand Canyon alert count: {gc_alert_count}, First alert title: {gc_first_alert_title}')

    # Final summary
    total_alerts = zion_alert_count + gc_alert_count
    execution_log.append('=== FINAL RESULTS ===')
    execution_log.append(f'Zion park code: {zion_code}, coordinates: ({zion_lat}, {zion_lon})')
    execution_log.append(f'Grand Canyon park code: {gc_code}, coordinates: ({gc_lat}, {gc_lon})')
    execution_log.append(f'(1) Driving distance from Zion to Grand Canyon: {distance_text}')
    execution_log.append(f'    Estimated travel time: {duration_text}')
    execution_log.append(f'(2) Total active alerts across both parks: {total_alerts}')
    execution_log.append(f'(3) First alert at Zion: "{zion_first_alert_title}"')
    execution_log.append(f'    First alert at Grand Canyon: "{gc_first_alert_title}"')

    return '\n'.join(execution_log)

evaluation_rules.noise_characteristics: findParks returns full park records for all parks in the state including descriptions, activities, topics, entrance fees, images, and dozens of other fields — agent must filter to find Zion/Grand Canyon and extract only the park code and coordinates. getParkDetails returns very verbose JSON with hours, contacts, addresses, images, activities, topics, entrance passes, fees, and more — agent must locate the GPS coordinates buried in this large object.

claims.description: Call findParks for Utah (stateCode='UT', limit=50), returns a large list of all Utah national parks with full metadata per park
claims.output_characteristics: List of ~10-15 parks each with dozens of fields: fullName, parkCode, description, activities, topics, entranceFees, images, latLong, url, addresses, contacts, operatingHours, etc.

claims.description: Filter Utah parks to find the one containing 'Zion' in its name; extract and remember its parkCode (expected: 'zion')
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter park list by fullName containing 'Zion', extract parkCode field

claims.description: Call getParkDetails for the Zion park code extracted in step 2; returns extremely verbose park record with all details
claims.output_characteristics: Single park object with 30+ fields including GPS coordinates buried among operatingHours, entranceFees, activities (50+ items), topics, images (multiple), contacts, addresses

claims.description: Extract and remember Zion's GPS entrance coordinates (latitude and longitude) from the verbose park details
claims.extraction_criteria: Locate latitude/longitude fields (or parse latLong string) from park detail object

claims.description: Call findParks for Arizona (stateCode='AZ', limit=50), returns large list of all Arizona national parks
claims.output_characteristics: List of ~10-15 Arizona parks, each with same verbose structure as Utah results — creates noise that must be filtered

claims.description: Filter Arizona parks to find the one containing 'Grand Canyon'; extract and remember its parkCode (expected: 'grca')
claims.extraction_criteria: Filter park list by fullName containing 'Grand Canyon', extract parkCode field

claims.description: Call getParkDetails for Grand Canyon using the park code from step 6; returns verbose park record
claims.output_characteristics: Same verbose structure as Zion details — GPS coordinates buried in large object with many irrelevant fields

claims.description: Extract Grand Canyon GPS coordinates from verbose details; use both Zion coordinates (from step 4) and Grand Canyon coordinates to call directions
claims.uses_signal: zion_lat, zion_lon (from step 4) as origin; gc_lat, gc_lon as destination
claims.output_characteristics: Directions response with routes array containing legs with distance.text and duration.text buried in polyline, steps, warnings, and other route metadata

claims.description: Call getAlerts for Zion park using park code remembered from step 2; returns list of all active alerts
claims.output_characteristics: Array of alert objects each with id, url, title, description, category, parkCode — variable count from 0 to 20+

claims.description: Call getAlerts for Grand Canyon using park code from step 6; tally total alerts across both parks and extract first alert title for each
claims.uses_signal: zion_alerts count from step 9, gc_park_code from step 6
claims.output_characteristics: Array of Grand Canyon alert objects; must combine with Zion count for total

_design_rationale: 1) WHY CHAIN IS LONG: The task requires 2 separate park searches (UT + AZ), 2 detail lookups, 1 directions call, and 2 alerts calls = minimum 7 tool calls, matching Medium difficulty. 2) WHERE NOISE COMES FROM: findParks returns full records for all parks in a state — each with 30+ fields including activities lists, images arrays, entrance fee arrays, and topic lists — agent must scan through all parks to find the matching name. getParkDetails is even more verbose, returning a single park's complete data including operating hours by day, all entrance fees, 50+ activities, contact info, addresses, and image galleries — GPS coordinates are just 2 fields among 30+. getAlerts returns multiple alert objects each with full descriptions and metadata. 3) SIGNAL EXTRACTION: Agent must extract 4 pieces of information: (a) Zion park code from step 1's noisy list, (b) Zion GPS coordinates from step 3's verbose object, (c) Grand Canyon park code from step 5's noisy list, (d) Grand Canyon GPS coordinates from step 7's verbose object. 4) HOW SIGNALS ARE USED LATER: Zion's GPS coordinates (extracted at step 3-4) are used as the ORIGIN in step 8's directions call — after 2 intervening noisy steps. Park codes (extracted at steps 2 and 6) are used in steps 9-10 for alerts retrieval. 5) WHY LOSING SIGNAL CAUSES FAILURE: If the agent loses Zion's GPS coordinates after step 4, it cannot compute the correct driving directions origin. If it confuses the two park codes, it gets alerts for the wrong parks. If it uses wrong coordinates (e.g., from Grand Canyon for both), the distance would be 0 or meaningless. 6) DIFFICULTY MATCH: 7+ tool calls, 4 signals to track across multiple noisy steps, moderate output sizes (dozens of fields per park record), signals used several steps after extraction — fits Medium level.

task: I'm planning a road trip through the US Southwest and want to compare three national parks: Zion (Utah), Grand Canyon (Arizona), and Joshua Tree (California). For each park, find its park code, then get its full details to extract the GPS coordinates of the park (latitude and longitude). Next, use those coordinates to get the current weather conditions at each park location. Then get the 7-day weather forecast for each park.
